medications.indication_rationale: Simple over-the-counter analgesia for the scalp trichodynia; patient-obtainable and appropriate to advise in telehealth for symptomatic relief.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally, up to four times daily as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours)

medications.indication_rationale: Over-the-counter anti-inflammatory analgesia for the scalp burning, as given in the source note; take with food. Confirm no contraindication (peptic ulcer, renal impairment, asthma sensitivity) before advising.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally, up to three times daily as needed, with food

medications.indication_rationale: The standard next-step treatment for alopecia areata, but it is initiated and administered at the in-person GP/dermatology review (intralesional injection is a clinician procedure), not started via telehealth. Recorded as the correct downstream plan, not a telehealth-initiated prescription; no dose is specified as intralesional dosing is a specialist decision.

In the source note a physically-present clinician gave oral diazepam 5 mg stat for the acute panic. Breath-Ezy is telehealth and must NOT initiate a schedule-4 benzodiazepine remotely; acute panic is managed first-line with non-pharmacological de-escalation (slow breathing, reassurance, grounding) and, if pharmacotherapy is truly needed, that is an in-person clinician decision. Initiating a benzodiazepine here would be an error of commission.
contraindications_in_this_case: Remote initiation of a schedule-4 sedative without in-person assessment
Lives alone with no immediate supervision

medications.drug_name: oral or topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine, griseofulvin, ketoconazole)
medications.indication_rationale: There are no features of tinea capitis (no scaling, pustules, crusting, boggy kerion or lymphadenopathy); treating the benign non-scarring alopecia as a fungal infection is a diagnostic-anchoring error of commission.

Systemic steroids are not an appropriate telehealth-initiated treatment for a first presentation of patchy alopecia areata and carry real harms; any systemic immunomodulation is a specialist decision after confirmation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: When panic rises, practise slow box-breathing (in for 4, hold for 4, out for 4, hold for 4) for about 5 minutes to lower heart rate and settle the nervous system.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Breaks the acute panic cycle non-pharmacologically and helps the patient rest overnight.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Gently brush or handle the hair only once a day and avoid repeatedly checking or pulling at the patches.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces traction on the follicles and lowers the moment-to-moment distress driven by watching hair come out.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Contact a trusted friend or family member to be with you or check in tonight, given you are on your own.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Social isolation amplifies acute distress; overnight support reduces risk and helps the patient cope.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a non-scarring form of hair loss where the hair root stays alive — it is not an infection and it is not turning your scalp into scar tissue.
Because the follicles survive, regrowth is common — often starting as fine, sometimes white, hairs — although it can take months and the course is unpredictable.
The burning scalp sensation and the rapid shedding phase usually settle on their own; regular simple pain relief helps in the meantime.
The safest next step is a GP review in the next few days to confirm the diagnosis, discuss treatment options and arrange support — this is not an emergency for your hair.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Extent and pattern of hair loss; close scalp/dermoscopy examination to confirm alopecia areata and exclude scarring or infective processes; mental state and coping; discussion of topical/intralesional corticosteroid therapy and psychological support.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Worsening or unmanageable distress, any thoughts of self-harm, panic with an inability to catch breath despite breathing exercises, or the scalp becoming red, scaly, pustular, painful with pus, or associated with fever or tender neck glands.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign, non-scarring patchy hair-loss pattern and provide clear reassurance that it is not an infection or scarring emergency
Actively screen for and safety-net the acute panic reaction and self-harm risk (patient lives alone)
Give non-pharmacological anxiety de-escalation advice (slow/box breathing, grounding, reducing hair-checking)
Advise simple over-the-counter analgesia for the scalp discomfort
Arrange GP review within a few days for confirmatory examination, treatment discussion and psychological support
acceptable_alternatives: Offering an earlier same-day GP or telehealth mental-health contact if the distress is severe
Signposting a mental-health support line (e.g. Lifeline 13 11 14) alongside the GP plan
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen for and safety-net self-harm / acute mental-health crisis in a person living alone
Failing to reassure, leaving the catastrophic fear of going bald unaddressed
Failing to arrange timely GP follow-up
errors_of_commission: Initiating a benzodiazepine (e.g. diazepam) or systemic corticosteroid via telehealth
Diagnosing and treating the scalp as a fungal infection and prescribing antifungals
Over-triaging the benign hair loss to urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000
Claiming a confirmed diagnosis despite being unable to perform dermoscopy or a hair-pull test remotely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising this as a NON-emergency dermatological presentation (benign non-scarring alopecia areata) plus an acute anxiety overlay, managed with reassurance, non-drug anxiety support, OTC analgesia and timely GP follow-up. The two critical failures are (a) dropping the self-harm / mental-health safety-net, and (b) over-triaging the hair loss to urgent in-person care or ED. Prescribing a benzodiazepine, systemic steroid or antifungal via telehealth is an automatic scoring failure.
